expectations.text: Asks at least one clarifying question OR explicitly states an assumption about the audience/situation
expectations.evidence: Opens with explicit diagnosis ('she has scar tissue'), then 'Sound right? If your read of her is different... tell me' offers a redirect.

expectations.text: Names a specific story arc or structure and explains why it fits
expectations.evidence: Explicitly picks No Easy Way over Rags to Riches with reasoning about three-strikes buyer pattern-matching smooth arcs as marketing fluff.

expectations.text: Addresses the VP's prior-bad-experience skepticism directly (does not paper over it)
expectations.evidence: Opens by stating she's 'skeptical of being sold to about analytics' and the opener line names her exact pattern out loud.

expectations.text: Recommends using the pilot customers as social proof or named-brand evidence
expectations.evidence: Picks messier pilot for arc story, uses other two as social proof with an honest 'still in the messy middle' tag for one.

expectations.text: Produces a structured artifact (outline, scene list, beat sequence) rather than a wall of prose
expectations.evidence: 30-minute structure broken into time-blocked sections, body is a five-beat No Easy Way arc with time estimates per beat.

expectations.text: Ends with a revision handle: a question, an ask for input, or a 'here's what only you can fill in' callout
expectations.evidence: Final section 'The one question you need to answer before Tuesday' names the specific Beat 3 detail only the user can supply.

expectations.text: Asks at least one clarifying question OR explicitly states an assumption about the audience/situation
expectations.evidence: Reframes the audience explicitly ('she is skeptical of adoption'), offers to pressure-test the user's existing deck.

expectations.text: Names a specific story arc or structure and explains why it fits
expectations.evidence: Suggests a 30-minute time-blocked structure but does not invoke any named story arc or named structural device.

expectations.text: Addresses the VP's prior-bad-experience skepticism directly (does not paper over it)
expectations.evidence: Opens with 'her real question isn't is your product good?' — explicitly recenters on adoption skepticism.

expectations.text: Recommends using the pilot customers as social proof or named-brand evidence
expectations.evidence: 'Pilot proof, told as stories' section, plus 'get the names of the three tools she bought' reconnaissance move.

expectations.text: Produces a structured artifact (outline, scene list, beat sequence) rather than a wall of prose
expectations.evidence: Time-blocked 30-minute structure with clear section breaks, preparation checklist, objections to rehearse.

expectations.text: Ends with a revision handle: a question, an ask for input, or a 'here's what only you can fill in' callout
expectations.evidence: Closes with 'share your current deck or talking points and I'll pressure-test them'.

expectations.text: Acknowledges the saturated/lazy-contrarian framing the user explicitly worries about
expectations.evidence: Multiple direct references: 'separates you from the lazy contrarians', 'thoughtful re-examination vs smug contrarian is whether you implicate yourself in Beat 2'.

expectations.text: Picks a structure that includes setbacks or earned credibility rather than pure optimistic arc
expectations.evidence: Explicitly chooses No Easy Way and explains: 'Not Man-in-a-Hole (too clean), not Rags-to-Riches (too triumphant)... your audience trusts a story with scars.'

expectations.text: Produces a beat-by-beat talk outline with time estimates or sequence (not just bullet themes)
expectations.evidence: Five labeled beats (1.5/3/4/4/2.5 min) each with content, purpose, and connection to a named tactic.

expectations.text: Plans specifics (numbers, before/after, named incidents) rather than abstract claims
expectations.evidence: Calls out exactly where specifics are needed, provides candidate shapes but flags them as placeholders for user's real specifics.

expectations.text: Ends with a reframing question or open challenge to the audience rather than a clean wrap
expectations.evidence: Beat 5 = 'three questions they walk out and run on their own org', closing line names the rethink theme.

expectations.text: Names which tactics or structural devices are being used and why
expectations.evidence: Tactic stack listed at top with rationale per tactic, each beat has a 'Why this beat is here' note, summary tactics block at bottom.

expectations.text: Acknowledges the saturated/lazy-contrarian framing the user explicitly worries about
expectations.evidence: Multiple direct callouts: 'lazy-contrarian trap', 'phrases that mark this genre' avoidance list.

expectations.text: Picks a structure that includes setbacks or earned credibility rather than pure optimistic arc
expectations.evidence: Reframes via an analytical/epistemic wedge rather than a narrative arc with setbacks. The 'decision-making pathology' framing is sharp but not structurally a setback-arc story.

expectations.text: Produces a beat-by-beat talk outline with time estimates or sequence (not just bullet themes)
expectations.evidence: Five time-blocked sections (0-2/2-5/5-10/10-13/13-15) with concrete content per section.

expectations.text: Plans specifics (numbers, before/after, named incidents) rather than abstract claims
expectations.evidence: Names a specific 2:14 AM opener, suggests quantifying hidden costs (e.g., '31% of senior engineering time'), insists on one extended case.

expectations.text: Ends with a reframing question or open challenge to the audience rather than a clean wrap
expectations.evidence: Closes with 'what evidence would change your mind, and when will you have it?'

expectations.text: Names which tactics or structural devices are being used and why
expectations.evidence: No named story arcs or structural devices invoked. Strong analytical framing but no narrative-craft vocabulary.

expectations.text: Leads with the news (the 18% miss) in the first line or paragraph, not buildup
expectations.evidence: Opens 'Q4 came in 18% below the number. I want to walk you through what I think happened...'

expectations.text: Explicitly chooses a failure frame and explains why
expectations.evidence: Frame stated up-front: 'Epic Fail as system breakdown + sign of the times', with reasoning: 'These are the two honest frames that don't require a scapegoat.'

expectations.text: Names both causes (external softening + internal pricing change) without scapegoating
expectations.evidence: Both causes named; pricing change owned at leadership level: 'this was not a sales execution problem.'

expectations.text: Ends with concrete asks or next steps, not just analysis
expectations.evidence: Three numbered Q1 actions: roll back pricing element, stand up weekly close-rate read, re-baseline Q1 forecast.

expectations.text: Tone is honest-not-panicked as requested
expectations.evidence: 'I'm not promising Q1 makes back what Q4 lost. I am telling you we now have a clearer read...' — calm, accountable.

expectations.text: Produces a memo structure (sections or clear paragraphs) the user can edit, not free-flowing prose
expectations.evidence: Clean sections: Where we were going in / What knocked us down / What we learned in the hole / The climb.

expectations.text: Leads with the news (the 18% miss) in the first line or paragraph, not buildup
expectations.evidence: 'The Bottom Line: We finished Q4 18% below target.' News up front.

expectations.text: Explicitly chooses a failure frame and explains why
expectations.evidence: Implicitly handles both external and internal cause but does not name or justify a failure frame as a craft decision.

expectations.text: Names both causes (external softening + internal pricing change) without scapegoating
expectations.evidence: Both causes named explicit 'This is on the strategy, not the execution.'

expectations.text: Ends with concrete asks or next steps, not just analysis
expectations.evidence: Three explicit asks: hold line on Q1 targets, air cover on pricing reversion, time on next week's agenda.

expectations.text: Tone is honest-not-panicked as requested
expectations.evidence: Confident but measured throughout.

expectations.text: Produces a memo structure (sections or clear paragraphs) the user can edit, not free-flowing prose
expectations.evidence: Standard memo headers and clean sections.

patterns: With-skill outputs consistently name a specific story arc (No Easy Way, Man in a Hole) and a tactic stack with rationale — the without_skill condition never does this, which is the central craft delta the skill teaches.

Quality gap is largest on Eval 1 (B2B pitch) where the explicit 'swap Rags to Riches for No Easy Way' move directly addresses the buyer's scar tissue. Without_skill picks up the adoption-skepticism insight but stops at advice, not narrative shape.

Quality gap is smallest on Eval 2 (contrarian talk) where the without_skill response is genuinely strong — it lands on an 'epistemic mistake' reframe that's intellectually competitive with the with_skill version. The differentiator is structural discipline, not insight.

With-skill runs cost ~2x time and ~1.7x tokens — driven by the SKILL.md read plus selective reference loads. Quality lift is large enough to justify the overhead for high-stakes communication; possibly not for routine pings.
non_discriminating_assertions: 'Leads with the news' and 'memo structure' both passed for both conditions on Eval 3 — these are general writing instincts Claude has without the skill, so they don't differentiate. Consider tightening to 'leads with the news in the first sentence' or replacing with assertions specific to deliberate failure-frame choice.
analyst_notes.tradeoffs: The skill consistently increases response length, tactic-naming, and time-to-output. Net win for users who want a craftable scaffold; possibly overkill for a user who just wants a polished one-shot draft.
